Name: information_theoretic_neural_compression
Title: Exploring the Fundamental Limits of Neural Network Compression through Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: The compression of neural networks can be fundamentally bounded by rate-distortion theory, and understanding these limits can lead to more efficient compression methods that preserve predictive sufficiency.
Related Work: While existing work has explored various techniques for compressing neural networks, such as pruning and quantization, few have approached the problem from an information-theoretic perspective. This proposal seeks to bridge that gap by applying rate-distortion theory to derive fundamental limits on neural network compression.
Abstract: This research proposal aims to investigate the application of rate-distortion theory to the compression of neural networks. By framing compression as a lossy source coding problem under a distortion constraint, we seek to derive fundamental limits on the achievable rate-distortion tradeoff for neural networks. Our approach will involve developing novel estimators for task-relevant information and exploring the relationship between compression and generalization through the lens of PAC-Bayes and MDL. The ultimate goal is to establish a theoretical framework for understanding the limits of neural network compression and to develop practical methods that approach these limits.
Experiments: Derive achievable rate-distortion bounds for neural networks using rate-distortion theory
Develop novel estimators for task-relevant information in neural networks
Evaluate the compression-performance tradeoff of existing methods against the derived theoretical limits
Explore the relationship between compression and generalization in neural networks through PAC-Bayes and MDL
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of neural networks may limit the applicability of rate-distortion theory
The development of novel estimators for task-relevant information may be challenging
The experimental evaluation of the proposed methods may require significant computational resources
